title: Psalms 124
subtitle: If the LORD Had Not Been on Our Side

sections.header: Verses 1–5 — “They Would Have Swallowed Us Alive”

heb.gloss: if not / were it not for
heb.paragraph: "If (lūlē) the LORD had not been on our side — let Israel say — if the LORD had not been on our side when people attacked us, they would have swallowed us alive" (vv.1–3). The counterfactual psalm: what IF God hadn’t been there? The flood would have engulfed us (v.4), the torrent swept us away (v.4), the raging waters overwhelmed us (v.5). Three images of drowning, all prevented by one fact: God was on our side.

notes.note: "If the LORD had not been on our side — they would have swallowed us alive." The double "if" (vv.1–2) emphasises the hypothetical: without God, destruction was certain.

"The flood would have engulfed us, the torrent would have swept over us, the raging waters would have swept us away." Three flood images, escalating: engulf → sweep → rage. All hypothetical — all prevented.

refs.note: 'If God is for us, who can be against us?'—Paul's question echoes the psalm's logic.
The LORD will fight for you; you need only to be still—deliverance from overwhelming enemies.
'Those who are with us are more than those who are with them'—Elisha's assurance.

hist.context: Psalm 124 is a communal thanksgiving for national deliverance, attributed to David. The congregation affirms together: 'If the LORD had not been on our side...' The enemies are described through flood imagery—raging waters that would have swept Israel away. This may recall the Exodus, specific military victories, or any moment when Israel faced annihilation.

sections.header: Verses 6–8 — “Our Help Is in the Name of the LORD”

heb.paragraph: "We have escaped like a bird from the fowler’s snare (faḥ); the snare has been broken, and we have escaped" (v.7). The image shifts from flood to trap. The bird is caught — then the snare breaks. Freedom is not the bird’s doing but the snare’s failure. God broke the trap. "Our help is in the name of the LORD, the Maker of heaven and earth" (v.8). The psalm ends with the same declaration as Psalm 121:2: help comes from the Creator.

notes.note: "Praise be to the LORD, who has not let us be torn by their teeth. We have escaped like a bird from the fowler’s snare." Teeth (predator) + snare (trapper): two danger-images, both escaped.
"Our help is in the name of the LORD, the Maker of heaven and earth." The pilgrimage’s creed: our help = God’s name = Creator’s power. The same formula as 121:2.

refs.note: Paul was rescued from the lion's mouth; the Lord will rescue him from every evil attack.
He will save you from the fowler's snare—the same trapping imagery used here.
The snare closing on all inhabitants of earth, from which believers must escape.

hist.context: The snare/trap imagery (v7) portrays enemies as hunters and Israel as prey. But the trap has broken and the bird has escaped! The closing confession—'Our help is in the name of the LORD, the Maker of heaven and earth'—became a standard liturgical formula, appearing in Jewish and Christian worship to this day. It connects deliverance to creation: the God who made all things can rescue from any enemy.

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: 1–2) emphasises the hypothetical: without God, destruction was certain…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVIf the Lord had not been on our side— let Israel say—ESVIf it had not been the LORD who was on our side\u2014 let Israel now say\u2014NIVif the Lord had not been on our side when people attacked us,ESVif it had not been the LORD who was on our side when people rose up against us,

rec.text: ""If the LORD had not been on our side — they would have swallowed us alive." The double "if" (vv.1–2) emphasises the hypothetical: without God, destruction was certain."

rows.label: The Snare Broken — 124:6–8
rows.text: Escaped like a BIRD. The snare broke. "Our help is in the NAME of the LORD."

hebtext.gloss: if not / were it not for
hebtext.note: "If (lūlē) the LORD had not been on our side — let Israel say — if the LORD had not been on our side when people attacked us, they would have swallowed us alive" (vv.1–3). The counterfactual psalm: what IF God hadn’t been there? The flood would have engulfed us (v.4), the torrent swept us away (v.4), the raging waters overwhelmed us (v.5). Three images of drowning, all prevented by one fact: God was on our side.

"We have escaped like a bird from the fowler’s snare (faḥ); the snare has been broken, and we have escaped" (v.7). The image shifts from flood to trap. The bird is caught — then the snare breaks. Freedom is not the bird’s doing but the snare’s failure. God broke the trap. "Our help is in the name of the LORD, the Maker of heaven and earth" (v.8). The psalm ends with the same declaration as Psalm 121:2: help comes from the Creator.

themes.note: Faith and Sovereignty peak: the counterfactual reveals total dependence. Without God: annihilation. With God: escape.
